prompt: Officers of the government, having conducted a great enumeration of the people for the purposes of apportionment, have furnished the particulars of that count — including the religion, nativity, and neighborhood of individuals — to other officers, who have used them to single out certain communities for heightened scrutiny, removal, or restriction of their liberties. Does the purpose of the census permit any use of its data beyond the apportionment it was designed to serve?
ground_truth_signal: Madison should reason from the original and sole constitutional purpose of the census — apportionment — and the principle that power granted for one purpose cannot be lawfully extended to another; he may invoke the danger of government surveillance of particular religious or ethnic communities as inimical to republican equality
response:
The enumeration prescribed by the Constitution serves a specific and limited purpose: the apportionment of representatives among the several states according to their respective numbers. This power, granted in the first article, second section, extends only so far as that constitutional object requires.

When officers of the government employ the particulars gathered in this enumeration for purposes beyond apportionment — particularly to identify and subject particular communities to special restrictions or scrutiny — they exceed the authority granted by the Constitution and violate fundamental principles of republican government.

First, the enumeration power must be construed strictly according to its constitutional purpose. The framers granted Congress authority to conduct a census for the single object of determining representation in the House of Representatives. To permit the use of census data for any other governmental purpose would be to expand federal power beyond the text and intent of the Constitution through administrative construction rather than constitutional amendment. Such a practice would render meaningless the careful enumeration of federal powers, for any power granted for one purpose could be stretched to serve any other purpose that ingenuity might devise.

Second, the use of census data to single out communities based upon religion directly contravenes the prohibition against religious establishments and the guarantee of free exercise. When government employs its enumeration power to catalog the religious affiliations of citizens, it creates the machinery of religious persecution. The same authority which might today use such information to restrict the liberties of one sect could tomorrow employ it against any other. No government consistent with religious liberty may compile lists of citizens according to their faith for purposes of differential treatment under law.

Third, such practices violate the principle of equal protection under law that underlies all republican government. When officers use census data to subject some citizens to heightened scrutiny based upon their nativity or neighborhood, they create classes of citizenship unknown to our Constitution. The enumeration counts persons, not to divide them into categories of greater or lesser liberty, but to ensure their equal representation in the councils of government.

The precedent established by such misuse of enumeration data would prove fatal to constitutional government itself. If census information may be employed for purposes beyond apportionment, then every future enumeration becomes an instrument of potential oppression. Citizens would rightfully refuse to cooperate with a count that might be used against them, rendering impossible the very apportionment the Constitution requires.

Experience has shown us that governments, once possessed of detailed information about their citizens, find endless pretexts for employing it. The officers who today use census data to restrict the liberties of particular communities will tomorrow discover new uses for such information that serve their convenience rather than constitutional purpose.

The remedy lies in strict adherence to constitutional limits. The enumeration serves apportionment and apportionment alone. Any other use of its data exceeds federal authority and threatens the liberties the Constitution was designed to protect.
